bibliographic_data.title: Attestation Waves: Platform Trust via Remote Power Analysis
bibliographic_data.abstract: Attestation is a strong tool to verify the integrity of an untrusted system. However, in recent years, different attacks have appeared that are able to mislead the attestation process with treacherous practices as memory copy, proxy, and rootkit attacks, just to name a few. A successful attack leads to systems that are considered trusted by a verifier system, while the prover has bypassed the challenge. To mitigate these attacks against attestation methods and protocols, some proposals have considered the use of side-channel information that can be measured externally, as it is the case of electromagnetic (EM) emanation. Nevertheless, these methods require the physical proximity of an external setup to capture the EM radiation. In this paper, we present the possibility of performing attestation by using the side-channel information captured by a sensor or peripheral that lives in the same System-on-Chip (SoC) than the processor system (PS) which executes the operation that we aim to attest, by only sharing the Power Distribution Network (PDN). In our case, an analog-to-digital converter (ADC) that captures the voltage fluctuations at its input terminal while a certain operation is taking place is suitable to characterize itself and to distinguish it from other binaries. The resultant power traces are enough to clearly identify a given operation without the requirement of physical proximity.

core_content.novelty_claim: The paper proposes utilizing remote power analysis for remote dynamic attestation, eliminating the physical proximity requirement of previous EM-based attestation proposals.
core_content.key_methodology: An analog-to-digital converter (ADC) captures voltage fluctuations at its input terminal during operation execution to distinguish between different binaries and identify operations without requiring physical proximity.
outcomes: Elimination of physical proximity requirement for remote attestation
Ability to capture power traces that can be used to verify the integrity of untrusted systems
core_content.full_text_markdown: Attestation is a powerful concept to verify the integrity of untrusted systems. However, recent attacks such as memory copy, proxy, and rootkit attacks have emerged, which can mislead attestation processes. To address these issues, this paper proposes using remote power analysis with an analog-to-digital converter (ADC) that captures voltage fluctuations during operation execution without requiring physical proximity. The ADC's captured power traces are sufficient to identify operations accurately.
